summary:
Local anesthetics block sodium ion influx via voltage-gated sodium channels, preventing depolarization necessary for neuronal firing; thus they inhibit transmission of sensory signals including pain. The effect results directly from interference with sodium channel function which stops action potential generation needed for neural conduction. To summarize Agent1’s reasoning into two sentences focusing only on critical clinical information related to how Lidocaine works as a local anesthetic:

Lidocaine inhibits sodium ion movement across neuron membranes by binding to voltage-gated sodium channels, thereby stopping the crucial steps involved in generating electrical impulses essential for signaling pain. By disrupting these processes locally within nerves, it achieves its analgesic effects without affecting distant parts of the body.
